title: Bengali Egg Curry

overview.description: Boiled eggs lightly fried and simmered in a rich Bengali onion, tomato and spice gravy.

ingredients: 3 tbsp mustard oil or neutral oil
2 medium potatoes, peeled and cut into large chunks
method: Place the eggs in a saucepan and cover with cold water.
Bring to the boil, then cook for 9–10 minutes.
Cool the eggs in cold water, peel them and make a few shallow cuts in the whites.
Season the eggs with ½ tsp turmeric and a little salt.
Heat 1 tbsp of the oil in a large frying pan.
Lightly fry the eggs until the outside develops a little colour.
Remove the eggs and set aside.
Add the remaining oil to the pan.
Add the potatoes and fry until lightly golden on the outside.
Remove and set aside.
Add the cumin seeds and bay leaf to the pan.
Add the sliced onion and cook until softened and golden.
Add the ginger and garlic and cook for 1–2 minutes.
Add the tomatoes, ground cumin, ground coriander, remaining turmeric, Kashmiri chilli powder and remaining salt.
Cook until the tomatoes break down and the oil begins to separate from the masala.
Add the water and potatoes.
Cover and simmer until the potatoes are almost tender.
Add the fried eggs and simmer gently for 5–10 minutes so they absorb some of the sauce.
Sprinkle with garam masala and fresh coriander.
Serve with rice.

learning.did-you-know: Egg curry is made in many different regional styles across India. Bengali versions often include potatoes and traditionally fry the boiled eggs before adding them to the gravy.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to build a layered masala gravy and how lightly frying boiled eggs changes their texture and flavour.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A regional egg curry that demonstrates how eggs can be the main protein in a substantial meal and introduces the children to Bengali spice combinations.
chapman-tips: Don't overcook the eggs; a slightly softer centre is more pleasant in the finished curry.
Making shallow cuts in the egg whites helps the sauce cling to them.
Frying the eggs is optional but adds flavour and a slightly firmer exterior.
Cook the onion slowly enough to develop colour without burning it.
The potatoes should be tender but still hold their shape.
Mustard oil gives the dish a more traditional Bengali character.
The curry is even better after resting for a short time before serving.
notes.batch-cooking: Best eaten fresh. The curry can be refrigerated and reheated gently, but the eggs may become firmer. Do not freeze the completed curry.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently and serve with rice or use the egg and sauce as a filling for a roti or paratha.
